Replacing a submersible well pump may seem like a daunting task, but with the right steps and tools, it can be done efficiently and effectively. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to replace a submersible well pump.

Step 1: Shut off the breaker to the pump.

Before starting any repair work, make sure to turn off the power source to the submersible well pump. You can do this by shutting off the breaker that supplies power to the pump.

Step 2: Locate and remove the top of the well head (cap).

Once the power has been turned off, remove the top of the well head or cap. This can typically be done using a wrench or pliers.

Step 3: Shine a bright flashlight into the well head.

Use a bright flashlight to illuminate the inside of the well head. This will help you see the condition of the well and gauge the extent of the work that needs to be done.

Step 4: Gather the tools needed.

To replace the submersible well pump, you'll need a "T" tool, pliers, wrenches, and a flashlight.

Step 5: Disconnect the electrical connections.

Using the "T" tool, disconnect the electrical connections to the pump. Make sure to turn off the breaker or remove the fuse before disconnecting the power.

Step 6: Remove the pump.

With the electrical connections removed, you can now remove the pump itself. Use the "T" tool and a cable to pull the pump up and out of the well.

Step 7: Remove the old pump.

After the pump has been removed, take out the old pump and dispose of it properly.

Step 8: Connect the new pump.

When connecting the new pump, it is crucial to have an electrically knowledgeable person or an electrical contractor make the connections. Once the new pump is connected, carefully lower it back into the well.

Step 9: Install the "T" tool.

After the pump is securely in place, install the "T" tool again. Use the cable to pull the tool up and out of the well.

Step 10: Reconnect the electrical connections.

Reconnect the electrical connections at the top of the well. If you are not comfortable doing this, consult an expert in electrical repair.

Step 11: Check for water.

Before installing the cap, check to see if you have water flowing from the well.

Step 12: Install the well cap.

Once you have confirmed that water is flowing properly, install the well cap securely.

Step 13: Check the water quality.

After completion, have the water checked for bacteria and softness by a certified water company. This will ensure that your water is safe and clean for consumption.

Replacing a submersible well pump may be a complex task, but with these steps and tools, you can do it right. Just remember to prioritize safety and consult an expert when necessary.

Avionics technicians are professionals who specialize in the maintenance, repair, and installation of electronic systems on aircraft. These systems include communication, navigation, and other electronic components that are crucial to the safety and functionality of an aircraft. Becoming an FCC licensed avionics technician is a significant achievement that requires dedication, focus, and hard work. Here are some steps to guide you on your journey:

1. Research the Field of Avionics to Learn More About the Work to See if the Field is a Good Match for You.

Before embarking on this journey, you need to understand what it means to be an avionics technician. Research the field and learn about the responsibilities and requirements of the job. You can find out more by speaking to other avionics technicians, reading articles, and reviewing industry publications.

2. Visit the Website of the FCC to Review the Description of General Radiotelephone Operators License and to Get an Overview of the Examination.

The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) is responsible for licensing avionics technicians. The General Radiotelephone Operator License (GROL) is a requirement for most avionics technician positions. Go to the FCC’s website and read the GROL description. Also, learn about the examination and the requirements for obtaining the license.

3. Browse the Internet and Explore Various Programs That Provide Technical Training in Preparation for a Position as an Avionics Technician if You Have no Experience Within the Field.

If you have no experience in avionics, participating in a training program is crucial. There are several programs that offer certificates or degrees in avionics. You can research online and find programs that are right for you.

4. Consider Taking Some Refresher Avionics Coursework If You are Already Working in the Field But Want to Become Certified and Licensed.

If you are already working in the field but want to become certified and licensed, consider taking some refresher courses. This will help you gain the knowledge and skills you need to pass the GROL examination. You can find refresher courses online or through local training centers.

5. Review the Various Sections of the Written Examination for the General Radiotelephone Operators License Available on the Website of the FCC.

The GROL examination consists of four elements, and you must pass all of them to obtain the license. Go to the FCC’s website and review each section of the examination. This will help you understand what to expect and what materials you need to study.

6. Review the Portion That Focuses on Written Element 1 of the General Radiotelephone Operators License on the FCC Website.

Written Element 1 of the GROL examination covers the knowledge necessary to correctly service or maintain communication equipment. Review the portion of this element on the FCC’s website in detail. This will help you understand the type of questions you will be asked and the materials you need to study.

7. Read Through the Overview Provided for Written Element 3 of the General Radiotelephone Operators License on the FCC Website.

This element covers the knowledge necessary to correctly service or maintain aviation equipment other than communication equipment. Review the description of this element on the FCC’s website in detail. This will help you understand the type of questions you will be asked and the materials you need to study.

8. Browse Through the FCC's Website to Discover That Sample Question and Answer Sheets Are Available for Perspective Examinees to Acquire in Preparation for the General Radiotelephone Operators License Examination.

The FCC website provides sample questions and answer sheets to help you prepare for the GROL examination. Take advantage of this resource and use it to practice answering questions that will be similar to those on the actual test.

9. Realize That Study Guides to Assist in Preparation for the General Radiotelephone Operators License Examination Can Be Obtained Via Links Provided on the FCC's Website.

The FCC website also provides links to study guides that can help you prepare for the GROL examination. Use these study guides to study efficiently and effectively. They will help you focus on the most critical information and increase your chances of passing the examination.

By following these steps, you'll be on your way to becoming an FCC licensed avionics technician. Remember to take your time and be thorough in your studies. With hard work and dedication, you'll achieve your goal in no time!

If you're feeling under the weather, you may be experiencing a fever. While a fever is a natural response to infection or illness, it can be uncomfortable and cause additional symptoms like chills, body aches, and fatigue. Fortunately, there are several steps you can take to get rid of a fever fast. Here's how:

1. Use over-the-counter medication for fevers caused by colds and flu.
Over-the-counter medications like acetaminophen or ibuprofen can help reduce fever and relieve other symptoms like pain and headaches.

2. Try taking a bath in lukewarm water. 
Taking a bath in lukewarm water can help bring down a fever and make you feel more comfortable. Make sure not to use cold water, as this can cause shivering and worsen the fever.

3. Drink water. 
Staying hydrated is essential when you have a fever. Drink plenty of water, and avoid caffeine and alcohol, as these can dehydrate you.

4. Take supplements to boost immune health. 
Supplements like vitamin C, zinc, and echinacea can help boost your immune system's response and help fight off the cause of the fever.

5. Administer "wet socks treatment" at home. 
This treatment involves wearing wet socks to bed, which can help improve circulation and stimulate the immune system.

6. Cool children's bodies if necessary. 
If your child has a fever, you can help bring it down by removing layers of clothing, giving them a lukewarm bath, and offering them plenty of fluids.

7. Stay as comfortable as possible. 
Resting, keeping cool, and wearing loose clothing can all help make you more comfortable while battling a fever.

8. Hydrate and eat mild foods to recover from gastrointestinal (GI) infection. 
If your fever is caused by a GI infection, it's crucial to stay hydrated and eat mild, easy-to-digest foods like plain crackers and toast.

9. Consume herbs known to fight fever. 
Herbs like ginger, garlic, and turmeric are all known for their immune-boosting and fever-fighting properties.

10. Take care not to spread infection. 
If you have a fever, take steps to prevent the spread of infection, like covering your mouth and nose while coughing and sneezing.

11. Keep a temperature log. 
If your fever doesn't resolve on its own, keeping a temperature log can help your doctor better understand your symptoms and determine the best treatment plan.

12. Seek medical advice. 
If your fever is high or accompanied by severe symptoms, it's important to seek medical advice.

13. Follow your doctor's instructions for viral infections. 
If your fever is caused by a viral infection, your doctor may recommend rest, fluids, and pain relievers.

14. Take antibiotics for bacterial infections. 
If your fever is caused by a bacterial infection, your doctor may prescribe antibiotics to help clear the infection.

15. Talk to your doctor about other causes of fever. 
If your fever persists or is accompanied by other symptoms like rash or joint pain, talk to your doctor about other potential causes.

16. Use a digital thermometer to take your temperature orally. 
Taking your temperature orally with a digital thermometer is a quick and easy way to monitor your fever.

17. Take your temperature in the armpit. 
If taking your temperature orally is difficult or uncomfortable, you can also take it in the armpit.

18. Decide which measurement method to use on a child. 
Oral, armpit, or rectal measurements are all options for taking a child's temperature. Talk to your doctor about which method is best for your child's age and symptoms.

19. Take the child’s temperature rectally with a digital thermometer. 
If taking your child's temperature rectally, be sure to sterilize the thermometer tip with rubbing alcohol first.

20. Interpret the results. 
Fever is generally defined as a temperature of 100.4°F (38°C) or higher.

21. Get vaccinated. 
Vaccines can help prevent certain illnesses that can cause fever.

22. Get enough sleep every day. 
Getting enough sleep is essential for a healthy immune system and can help lower your risk of getting sick.

23. Eat a healthy diet. 
Eating a balanced, nutritious diet can help your body fight off infections and illnesses.

24. Avoid contact with germs. 
Taking steps to avoid contact with germs can help reduce your risk of getting sick.

25. Reduce your stress levels. 
Stress can weaken your immune system's response, so finding ways to reduce stress is crucial for overall health and wellness.

By following these tips, you can help get rid of a fever fast and get back to feeling your best.

How to Use the OLED Touch Screen Bar on a Mac

The OLED Touch Screen Bar on a Mac is a convenient feature that allows you to easily access important system functions and customize your Touch Bar. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to use the OLED Touch Screen Bar on a Mac:

Find the system functions.

The OLED Touch Screen Bar on a Mac displays important system functions. To view these functions, tap the < button on the Touch Bar.

Press and hold the Brightness button.

To adjust the brightness of your Mac's display, press and hold the Brightness button. Then, drag your finger left and right to adjust the brightness.

Press and hold the Volume button.

To adjust the volume on your Mac, press and hold the Volume button. Then, drag your finger left and right to adjust the volume.

Tap the Mute button to mute your Mac.

To quickly mute your Mac, tap the Mute button on the Touch Bar.

Tap the Siri button to start Siri.

To activate Siri, tap the Siri button on the Touch Bar.

Tap the < button to expand the system functions.

To view additional system functions, tap the < button on the Touch Bar.

Tap the < and > buttons to move back and forth.

To navigate between different screens in the Touch Bar, tap the < and > buttons.

Tap the Sort button to change the sorting.

To sort your files and folders, tap the Sort button on the Touch Bar.

Tap the Eye button to open Quick Look.

To quickly preview a file, tap the Eye button on the Touch Bar to open Quick Look.

Tap the Share button to share your selected files.

To easily share files and folders, tap the Share button on the Touch Bar.

Tap the Tag button to add tags to files and folders.

To add tags to your files and folders, tap the Tag button on the Touch Bar.

Tap the X to close.

To close a window or app, tap the X button on the Touch Bar.

Tap a favorite website to load it.

To quickly access a favorite website, tap the website's icon on the Touch Bar.

Tap the < and > to go back and forth.

To navigate between different web pages, tap the < and > buttons on the Touch Bar.

Tap the Search bar to enter a search or website address.

To search or enter a website address, tap the Search bar on the Touch Bar.

Tap the New Tab button to open another tab.

To open a new tab in your web browser, tap the New Tab button on the Touch Bar.

Tap the Smiley button.

To access emojis, tap the Smiley button on the Touch Bar.

Tap the Category button.

To browse different categories of emojis, tap the Category button on the Touch Bar. Then, swipe left and right to view the available characters. Finally, tap an emoji to add it to whatever you're typing.

Customize your Touch Bar.

To customize your Touch Bar, open the app that you want to customize the Touch Bar for. Then, click the View menu and select Customize Touch Bar. From here, click and hold an item you want to add and drag it to the bottom of the screen. Then, drag the item left and right in the Touch Bar and place it in the desired location. To remove an item from your Touch Bar, click and drag it out of the Touch Bar.

Tap Done in the Touch Bar when you're finished customizing.

When you're finished customizing your Touch Bar, tap Done to save your changes.

Customize your Control Strip.

To customize your Control Strip, click the Apple menu and select System Preferences. Then, select Keyboard and click the Keyboard tab. From here, click Customize Control Strip and click and hold an item that you want to add. Next, drag the item to the bottom of the screen and continue dragging until it appears in the Touch Bar. Finally, release the mouse when it is in the desired position. To remove an item from your Touch Bar, move your cursor to the Touch Bar, click and hold an item, and drag it to the trash.

Add your fingerprint to Touch ID.

To use Touch ID, click the Apple menu and select System Preferences. Then, select Touch ID and click Add a fingerprint. Type your account password and place your finger on the Touch ID sensor when prompted. Lift and place your finger again in a slightly different position and repeat this process until the scan is complete. Finally, click the checkboxes for the services you want to use and unlock your Mac with your fingerprint.

That's it! With these tips, you can use the OLED Touch Screen Bar on your Mac to streamline your workflow and customize your Touch Bar.

Talkspot.com is a platform that allows you to build a free website in just a few simple steps. If you are a beginner and don't have any programming knowledge, this tutorial is perfect for you. Follow the instructions below, and you'll have your website up and running in no time.

Part 1: Quick Start

1. Go to http://www.talkspot.com and click on "Get Started Now."
2. Enter your setup information, such as email, choose a password, and pick a name for your website. This is how TalkSpot sets up your site for you to begin with.
3. Log in and get started.

Part 2: Adding a New Webpage

1. Once you sign in, you'll notice a bar that appears at the top of your webpage. Each of these squares represents a webpage template or the layout of a webpage.
2. Add a new page to your website by clicking on the "+" icon on the right side of the bar.
3. Give the new page a descriptive name.
4. Add something to the page. To do this, click on the greenie located at the top center of your web page.
5. Pick the "Text" blob and click on it.
6. Click on the pencil located at the top right corner of your newly created text blob.
7. Add some content to the text blob and click "Save."
8. To add a second text blob, repeat steps 4-6.
9. Review your work.

Part 3: More Pages

1. Repeat Steps 2-8 from Part 2 to add additional pages to your website.
2. That's it! Your website is now up and running.

Using Talkspot.com to build a free website is an easy and affordable way to create a web presence for your personal, professional, or business needs. Just follow the steps outlined in this tutorial, and you'll be on your way to building your website in no time. Good luck!

Fallout shelters have been around since the beginning of the Cold War era of the 1950s. Their purpose was to protect people from the deadly effects of a nuclear blast and radiation after the detonation of a bomb. Nowadays, building a fallout shelter is a smart thing to do, as natural disasters and other unexpected emergencies can happen at any time. In this article, we'll guide you on how to build a fallout shelter from scratch.

Part One: Planning

1. Think about what kind of shelter you want to build. There are two types of fallout shelters you can build: in-house and freestanding. If you have a basement or a concrete cellar, you can use your space to build an in-house shelter. If you prefer a freestanding structure, then you need to choose a location where you can dig an underground trench.

2. Print out the instructions for the shelter you want to make and cross out each step when completed. Make sure that you have all the equipment and materials on hand before you begin.

3. Gather the tools which include shovels and saws. You may need electrical equipment, lighting, and filters if you plan to spend more extended periods in the shelter.

4. Assign different tasks to different individuals according to their strengths and weaknesses. If you have multiple people available to help build the fallout shelter, delegate tasks according to their strengths. For example, if someone is excellent at carpentry, assign them the task of constructing the shelter's interior.

5. Choose an area that has no flammable material around it. You don't want to build a fallout shelter near any flammable material, especially if you plan to have electrical equipment in it.

Part Two: Construction

1. When digging, pile up the soil at least five feet away from the trench. You'll use this soil later to cover the shelter.

2. Dig the trench as deep as you want to, but remember that the lower it is, the more room you have and it may improve blast protection. The typical depth range is between 6 to 8 feet below the ground.

3. When finished digging, place the logs/poles on top of the trench. These logs will serve as the shelter's framework.

4. Now that you have the logs on top of the trench, MAKE SURE TO cover any cracks with, for example, cloth or leaves. This step is crucial as any gaps or cracks let dirt and radiation into the shelter.

5. When you are absolutely positive that there is no way for the dirt to get into the living space, place the soil you dug out on the logs, so that the layer of earth is at least 18 inches high. This layer will further insulate the shelter and provide additional protection from radiation.

6. Build a toilet of some sort on a space separated from the rest of the shelter with at least a blanket or a cloth of some kind. This area should be well-ventilated and have easy access to change out waste.

7. Make beds of some kind. Beds don't have to be fancy but equip them with essential bedding like sheets and pillows. Bunks are an excellent option to help maximize space.

8. Since no one likes to be trapped inside a fallout shelter if a fire happens, make sure you have at least two different exits in your fallout shelter. This should be part of your design and be included in the construction process, even before you start digging.

In conclusion, building a fallout shelter can save your life in an emergency situation. This guide will help you construct a basic fallout shelter from scratch. Remember, always put safety first and consider professional help if you're unsure of what to do. Stay safe!

How to Win Bullshit: Be Honest and Strategic

Bullshit, also known as Cheat, is a fun and challenging card game that requires players to lie and bluff their way to victory. But if you want to come out on top, it's important to be both honest and strategic. Here are some tips for mastering the game.

1. Arrange your cards strategically

Instead of ordering your cards from A to K, arrange them in the order that you anticipate the values will come to you. This can help you keep track of what cards you have and what you need to bluff.

2. Choose your lies carefully

If you have to lie, choose the card that comes latest in the sequence. For example, if the pile is currently on 9's, but you only have 4's, it's better to say that you have 9's than to say you have 4's and risk getting caught.

3. Keep track of the game

Pay attention to how many players are in the game and adjust your strategy accordingly. The sequence of cards will be different with different numbers of players, so be prepared to adapt.

4. Always keep a straight face

Maintain a constant expression throughout the game to avoid giving away any tells. This can be especially important if you're playing with newbies, who may be more likely to pick up on your mood.

5. Play it safe when necessary

If someone has just played their last card, don't be afraid to call BS unless you're absolutely sure they're telling the truth. It's better to play it safe and avoid getting burned.

6. Slip in a fifth card

If you have all four of a kind, try slipping in a fifth card that you won't need. This can throw off your opponents and give you an extra edge.

7. Be prepared to defend yourself

If you're about to be caught and the pile is large and messy, try to stick your card in between some other cards so that if someone BS's you and turns over the top card, you can claim they messed up the order.

8. Laugh it off

If you do get caught, don't get too upset. Laugh it off and make a smug face, as if you were just playing around.

9. Keep your cards hidden

Always keep your cards in a way that other people can't tell how many cards you have. This keeps your bluffing game strong and prevents your opponents from figuring out what you're up to.

10. Smile suspiciously

If you're playing with newbies, you can try making a tiny, suspicious smile when you have the right card. This may throw them off and make them more likely to believe your bluff.

11. Know what you need

Always keep track of what card you need next and whether or not you have it. This can help you stay ahead of the game and bluff more effectively.

12. Say the right thing

If it's your turn to play a jack and you have a jack, you can play it and say, "One eleven, I mean jack." This can further confuse your opponents and give you an extra edge.

By following these tips, you'll be well on your way to mastering Bullshit and taking home the victory every time.

How to Use Craigslist: Become Proficient in Searching Your Local Craigslist

Craigslist is a website that allows people to buy, sell, rent, and find jobs and services. With its large user base and active community, it's a great place to find bargains or promote your own items. In this article, we'll show you how to use Craigslist like a pro.

Part 1: Peruse the Different Sections of the Site

Craigslist has many different sections, including housing, personals, jobs, for sale, and community. You can easily navigate through these sections by selecting the category you are interested in from the list on the main page. Once you have selected a category, you can use the search bar to locate specific items or services.

Part 2: Decide How You Would Most Benefit from Posting on Craigslist

If you want to post on Craigslist, you need to decide what you want to sell, rent, or offer. Once you have decided what you want to post, make sure you are ready to check back regularly for responses, and post only when you are ready to do so.

Part 3: Post Your Listing

To post your listing, find the “Post” link in the upper right-hand corner of the page. Click on this link, and then select the category your listing falls under. Answer any additional questions for that category by selecting the radio button that describes your post. Next, enter text for your post, including a posting title and description. Enter your personal email, type your email address again and click “Continue.” You can upload up to 12 images, if it applies to your listing. Confirm your email and post.

Part 4: Check Your Email and Respond to Inquiries

Check your email for a Craigslist email. Only respond to specific inquiries that are related to your listing and don’t give your address information out to anyone unless you have contacted them and you are certain of your safety.

Part 5: Become Familiar with What Items Are Best for Buying and Selling on Craigslist

Craigslist is a great place to find bargains on items such as furniture, electronics, and clothing. However, it's also a great place to sell items such as antiques, collectibles, and unique items. Comparison shop before you buy and determine whether the seller is willing to bargain on the post.

Part 6: Be Specific with Your Item Description and Images When You Are Selling

When you are selling an item, be specific with your item description and images. Offer delivery service if you want to get rid of an item quickly, and try to speak to the person and confirm the address and time before you buy or sell.

Part 7: Repost Your Ad Every 48 Hours to Remain Relevant on the Chronological List

To remain relevant on the chronological list, repost your ad every 48 hours. This will help ensure that your ad remains visible and that you get the exposure you need to sell your item.

By following these tips, you'll soon become proficient in searching your local Craigslist, posting listings, and finding great deals. Happy hunting!

How to Learn Spanish Reflexive Verbs

Spanish is a beautiful language with intricate grammar rules. Among these rules are reflexive verbs, which can be challenging to learn. However, with practice and the right approach, you can master the art of Spanish reflexive verbs. Here's how:

1. Recognize reflexive verbs

The first step towards learning Spanish reflexive verbs is to recognize them. These verbs are characterized by the reflexive pronoun se attached to the end of the infinitive form of a verb. For example, the reflexive form of the verb lavar (to wash) is lavarse (to wash oneself). Once you can identify reflexive verbs, you can begin to learn how to use them correctly in sentences.

2. Learn the reflexive verbs that are equivalent of get+certain prepositions in English

Many reflexive verbs in Spanish are the equivalent of the English verb get with certain prepositions. Some examples include:

    Acostarse (to go to bed)
    Levantarse (to get up)
    Darse cuenta de (to realize)
    Ponerse de acuerdo (to agree)
    Sentirse bien/mal (to feel good/bad)

By learning these verbs, you can expand your vocabulary and learn some useful phrases for everyday conversations.

3. Know common reflexive verbs for daily routine

Another set of reflexive verbs that you should learn are the ones related to daily routine tasks. These verbs include:

    Lavarse (to wash oneself)
    Peinarse (to comb one's hair)
    Bañarse (to take a bath)
    Vestirse (to get dressed)

By incorporating these verbs into your daily practice, you will not only remember them better, but also improve your Spanish fluency.

4. Recognize that all Spanish verbs have a reflexive counterpart

Another important thing to remember is that all Spanish verbs have a reflexive counterpart, even if they might not be frequently used in that form. For example, the verb hablar (to talk) can also be reflexive as hablarse, which means to speak to oneself. Knowing this fact can help you approach any verb in a more comprehensive way and increase your understanding of the language.

5. Understand when the verb-derived action is exaggerated

Sometimes reflexive verbs in Spanish are formed by an action that is exaggerated to a certain extent, causing a slight change in the verb's meaning. For example, the verb reír (to laugh) can become reírse (to burst laughing), implying that something was extremely humorous.

6. Identify reflexive verbs formed by the use of the verb ponerse and volverse

The verbs ponerse and volverse can be used to form reflexive verbs in Spanish. The verb ponerse is used to express a change in one's physical appearance or mental state, while volverse implies a consequence of an action that occurred in the past. For example, "él se puso contento" means "he became happy", while "me volví loco buscando mis llaves" means "I went crazy looking for my keys."

7. Understand that volverse can be used to express total transformation or imply professional expertise

Volverse can also be used to indicate a total transformation. For example, "con los años se volvió más sensible" means "over the years, he became more sensitive." Additionally, some reflexive verbs are followed by nouns to indicate that someone has become a professional in a certain area. For example, "él se hizo médico" means "he became a doctor".

By following these tips and practicing regularly, you can improve your understanding and proficiency in Spanish reflexive verbs. So, don't hesitate to dive in and continue learning!

If your employer, college, or university provides you with a search engine or directory, begin there. If you have access to a library database of research articles, such as EBSCOhost, start there.Library databases provide you with access to peer-reviewed research, which is the gold standard for academic study. “Peer-reviewed” means that top experts in the field have reviewed the research to make sure it is accurate, trustworthy, and informed before publishing it. Even if you’re just trying to learn something for your own personal benefit, academic research will provide you with the most up-to-date, reliable information.


You can usually access these databases through your home library’s website. Some academic and universities libraries may require a password if you are accessing them remotely (from somewhere other than in the library itself).
If you don’t have access to a library, try using Google Scholar for your searches. You can find academic research through this search engine, and Google Scholar will show you where you can find free copies of the articles online.;
, Depending on the area of your research, you have several options for online databases specific to your field. For example, if you are looking for research on education, the ERIC (Education Resources Information Center) is sponsored by the United States Department of Education and provides peer-reviewed research and informational materials on education topics.If you’re looking for medical or scientific research, PubMed, sponsored by the United States National Library of Medicine, is a great place to start., If you have access to a library, make an appointment to speak with your reference librarian. These people are specially trained in helping you access the best research and knowledge available.They can help you find sources and also help you determine whether sources are credible.

, Search engines crawl the web indexing pages by reading the words and phrases that appear on those pages. From there, the process is automated. Each search engine has an algorithm that’s used to rank results for specific searches. This means that no human is vetting the accuracy of the results. The “top” result is simply the result of an algorithm. It’s not an endorsement of the content or quality of the result.Most search engines can be “gamed” by savvy websites in order to ensure their content comes up first. Moreover, each search engine has its own algorithm, and some tailor their results based on your browsing history. So the “top” result on Google will not necessarily be the “top” result on Yahoo, even with the exact same search phrasing.Be aware that simply because you find information online doesn’t make it credible or authoritative. Anyone can make a webpage, and the amount of poor, unverified, and just plain wrong information often outweighs the good stuff online.To help you sift through the useless stuff, talk to your teacher or librarian, and use library or academic search engines when possible.

, For any given inquiry, there are an almost limitless number of potential word and phrase choices you could enter into a search engine. Therefore, it’s important to think carefully about what you hope your search will find, as well as try multiple different search combinations.


If you’re using an academic search engine, such as your library’s search feature, try using a combination of keywords and Boolean Operators, or words you can use to narrow down your search: AND, OR, and NOT.For example, if you are doing research on feminism in China, you might run a search for “feminism AND China.” This will return results that include both of those topic keywords.
You can use OR to run searches for related keywords. For example, you could search for “feminism OR feminist OR social justice.” This would return results that contain one or more of those terms.
You can use NOT to exclude keywords from your search. For example, you could search for “feminism AND China NOT Japan.” You would not get any results that included Japan.


You can use quote marks to search for full phrases. For example, if you want to search for academic performance, you would search for the whole phrase inside quotation marks: “academic performance.” Be aware, though, that using quotation marks will kick out any result that isn’t an exact match. For example, you would not get results about “school performance” or “academic functioning” because they are not worded exactly the way you searched.
Use specific keyword phrases to locate the most relevant information. For example, if you are looking for information social welfare expenditures in the U.S., you’re more likely to get the results you want by searching for “total yearly amount spent on welfare programs in U.S.” than searching for “welfare,” which would bring up definitions of welfare, types of welfare in other countries, and thousands more results you don’t want. Be aware, though, that you can’t always find information like this -- the more words you enter, the fewer results you’re likely to get.
Use alternate words or keyword phrases to locate additional research sources. For example, if you are researching “welfare,” consider using “safety net” or “social programs” or “public assistance” in place of “welfare” to find different results. In many cases, your word choice might unintentionally bias your results, since terms like “welfare” are often politically loaded. Using a wider variety of terms ensures that you’ll be exposed to a broader — and therefore potentially less biased — set of sources.

, If you’re researching a topic about which you’re relatively uninformed, begin your search with broad terms, then use the information culled from that first search to begin narrowing your search.


For example, in your search for “total yearly amount spent on welfare programs in U.S.,” you’ll quickly discover that there are several different public assistance programs, such as Temporary Assistance for Needy Families (TANF) and Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP). Use that information to decide which program(s) you’re interested in, and then perform a new (more specific) search, such as “total yearly SNAP expenditures in U.S.”

, Perhaps the most difficult — and important — task in internet research is ensuring the sources you select are credible. Generally, you want to prioritize information from government sources, academics, and nationally recognized news organizations.Government sources will often have “.gov” somewhere in the webpage. For example, the United States Department of State’s website is www.state.gov. The official website for Australia’s Department of Defence is www.defence.gov.au.
Websites that end in .edu belong to colleges and universities. However, you do need to be careful with .edu sites, because often faculty and students can run personal webpages that will have the .edu extension, but the information there may not be vetted by the university.It’s better to find academic sources through an academic database or search engine, like EBSCOhost or Google Scholar.
Websites that end in .org belong to non-profit organizations. While some of these are highly credible, some are not. Anyone can purchase a website with a .org extension. Check these sites carefully, and don’t rely on them as your sole source of information if you can avoid them.Major news sources such as The Guardian, CNN, and Al Jazeera tend to be credible, but you also need to make sure you’re reading a factually based article and not an opinion piece. Many news sites also have blogs and editorial sites where people can state their opinions, which aren’t necessarily backed up by facts.

, Don’t limit yourself to the first few results in the search engine. Look beyond the first page of search results to find information for your research.While it’s impossible to view all of the results for most searches, it’s important to view at least several pages of results in order to ensure you’re not missing important information. Because of search engine optimization, if you’re using a regular search engine like Google or Yahoo, the first several pages might contain the links that were most effectively promoted, not the ones with the best information., Go to the original source whenever possible.


For example, if you are writing a report on penguins, you could start with the Wikipedia page on Penguins. Scrolling to the References section would show you several peer-reviewed academic journal articles on penguins, along with references to book chapters by academic publishers. Look at those sources for more authoritative information.

, During your research, you will find many statements online, but not all of them are true or useful. Some sources will not cite any references, or they may twist the reference to say something other than what it originally stated. Don’t take anything at face value. Particularly when the website reporting a fact or statistic is questionable, you should attempt to find the original source.


For example, if you’re doing research on changes in welfare expenditures during the past 20 years, there’s no reason to trust Yahoo answers, a blog, or any secondary source. Most credible sources will note that they’re using data from federal agencies. Therefore, it’s usually better to search for the original government data sources and cite them directly, rather than citing a page that is itself just reporting (possibly incorrectly) the data.
Citing the original source will also make your own research more authoritative and credible. For example, it is much more impressive to your teacher if you cite an article from the National Institutes of Health (a US government source) than if you cite an article from webMD -- even if they have the same information. If you can cite the original scholarly research that produced the information you're discussing, that's even better.

, If you can’t find the original source for a fact, your best bet is to verify the fact on multiple, credible sites.


No matter what information you’re seeking, if you can’t find a single official source, it’s advisable not to trust a piece of information until you find identical information on several independent sites. So, for example, if you can’t find an original source for SNAP expenditures in 1980, enter the data you found into a search engine to ensure that the same number is reported on multiple sites and that those sites are not all citing the same (potentially erroneous) source.

, For example, the Mayo Clinic website is owned by the Mayo Clinic, one of the most prestigious hospitals in the world. It is a not-for-profit organization, so it is not out to make money from its content. Its articles are written by medical professionals. These are good clues that information you find on this site will be credible. On the other hand, a “health” website that has a storefront or lots of ads, and doesn’t have any institutional or professional affiliations, won’t be as credible.


If you’re using an academic database, check out who published the article or book. Texts from prestigious journals, such as the New England Journal of Medicine, and books from academic publishers like the Oxford University Press, carry more weight than sources from less-known publishers.
If you’ve never heard of a source, the first place to look is the “About Us” (or similar) portion of the website. If that doesn’t provide you with a good idea of who’s producing the web page, try conducting an internet search for the site itself. Often news articles, Wikipedia entries, and the like that reference a source will include information about its affiliation(s), ideology, and funding. When all else fails, consider using a web domain search engine to discover who owns the website. However, if you’ve had to go to that length, chances are good that the site is too obscure to be trusted.

, Unfortunately, many internet sources will not list an author. If you are searching online for peer-reviewed research, however, you will usually find sources with named authors. Look at their credentials.For example, does this person have education in his/her field? Neil deGrasse Tyson has a Ph.D. in Astrophysics from the prestigious Columbia University, so it’s likely that what he says about astrophysics is credible and authoritative (meaning trustworthy and up-to-date).On the other hand, an amateur star-watcher’s blog will not be authoritative, even if the information is accurate.
Has the author written anything else on the topic? Many authors, including journalists and academic scholars, have areas of specialty and have spent years studying and writing about these topics. If the author has written many other articles on the same area, this makes him/her more credible (especially if those articles are peer-reviewed).
If there is no author, is the source credible? Some sources, particularly government sources, will not list an author. However, if the source you are getting the information from is authoritative -- such as an article on chickenpox from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention -- the absence of an author isn’t cause for concern on its own.

, It’s important to make sure that your information is as up-to-date as possible, especially if you’re research a medical or scientific topic. Scientific consensus changes with the presence of new studies and information. Check when the article or website was published. Being more than five years old isn’t necessarily bad, but look for the most recent articles you can find for the best shot at updated information.For example, if you were writing a research paper on treatments for cancer, you wouldn’t want to use only articles from the 1970s, even if they were published in prestigious academic journals.

, There are many sources out there that claim to be fact-based but aren’t. Websites that appear to have a clear agenda are usually not good sources, because they may ignore or misrepresent evidence that disagrees with their position.


Look for the site’s sources. A credible internet site will cite its sources. A really great site may even link out to the original research articles so you can track them down. If you can’t find any references for the information provided, or if the references are out of date or poor quality, it’s a good sign that your site isn’t reliable.
Watch for bias. Highly emotional language, inflammatory rhetoric, and informal writing are all signs of potential bias in your source. Most academic writing tries to steer clear of these and aim for impartiality and objectivity as much as possible. If your website uses emotional language like “Manipulative big pharma companies are out to keep you broken and unhealthy to line their own pockets!” it’s a good sign that there is bias present.
Review each website for grammatical errors and broken links. If the website is credible and reliable, grammar and spelling should be accurate and all links should take you to the appropriate landing page. Websites with numerous grammatical errors and broken links may be copying their information from another source or may not be legitimate.

, In order to avoid the same errors made by inaccurate sites, you should always document your sources. This will allow you to return to them later, if necessary, and will allow others (when applicable) to verify your sources themselves.


Bibliography entries for webpages traditionally consist of the author of the web article or webpage (if available), the title of the article or page, the name of the site, the site’s web address, and the date on which you accessed the article or page.

, Just because a source is there today doesn't mean it's going to be there tomorrow. In order to guard against making your research irrelevant, consider your options for preserving web pages.


The simplest way to save a webpage as you see it today is to print a hard copy or save it as a PDF.This will allow you to refer back to the page, even if it's moved or deleted.
Since a hard copy or PDF version will only be available to you, you should periodically check the links in your research if it is published on the web. If you discover a web page has been deleted or moved, you can keyword search for its new location in a search engine or check to see if it was archived by Archive.org's Wayback Machine, which preserves web pages as they previously displayed., There are numerous free web browser features, apps, and services that can help you save your sources quickly and organize them easily.


Using the bookmarks feature of your web browser is the simplest way to save sources. Rather than saving every source in the parent “Bookmarks” folder, consider creating subfolders for specific topics. For example, if you’re researching welfare, you might want to create a folder for “Welfare” in “Bookmarks” and then maybe even create more folders within “TANF,” “SNAP,” etc.

, Beyond simple bookmarking features and apps, more advanced research software and services can help you create your own personal repository of sources.


Numerous services and apps have made it possible to sync sources to the cloud, capture images of web pages as they appear on the day you accessed them, add keywords to sources, etc.
Many of these services, such as Zotero, are freeware created by academics and other open-source advocates. Others, such as Pocket, offer some services for free and charge for others. If you need functions beyond your web browser’s standard bookmarking features, consider using one of these sources to make organizing your sources easier.

If you're traveling with an infant or toddler, it's important to have an emergency kit on hand in case of any unexpected situations. One effective way to do this is to pack a car's underseat drawer with all the necessary items that your child may need during travel. In this article, we'll walk you through the steps of packing a car's underseat drawer for an infant or toddler child's emergency needs.

1. Make a list of your child's most needed items first.

The first step is to make a list of all the items your child would need during travel. This helps to ensure that you don't forget anything important. The list may include diapers, baby wipes, first aid kit, water, snacks, toys, and extra clothes.

2. Pick a suitable area in the car for the underseat drawer.

The next step is to find a suitable area in the car for the underseat drawer. Look for areas that are easily accessible and safe for your child. It's also important to ensure that the drawer doesn't block any important circuits or wires in the car.

3. Realize that you'll want to keep this emergency kit in the car.

Keep in mind that you'll likely want to keep this emergency kit in the car at all times. This way, you'll always be prepared for any unexpected situations that may arise during travel.

4. Tailor the underseat area to suit the season first.

Depending on the season, you may need to tailor the underseat area to suit your child's needs. For instance, during winter, you may want to include blankets, extra winter wear, and a warm drink.

5. Tailor the drawer to suit the time of day when the most travel will be done.

Also, consider the time of day when the most travel will be done. For instance, if you'll be traveling during the day, you may need to include sunscreen, sunglasses, and hats.

6. Tailor the drawer to suit the child's age.

It's important to consider your child's age when packing the drawer. For instance, infants may need more diapers and baby wipes, while toddlers may need more snacks and toys.

7. Find a suitable container for your kit.

Find a suitable container for your emergency kit that can fit into the underseat drawer comfortably. You can use a diaper bag or backpack for this purpose.

8. Beware of supplies that expire.

It's important to check the expiry date of any supplies in your emergency kit regularly. You don't want to use expired items in an emergency situation.

9. Stock only small amounts of things you'll need.

Only stock small amounts of the items that your child will need during travel. This leaves enough space for other items and ensures that the bag or backpack stays lightweight.

10. Look for quick cleanup options.

It's important to include quick cleanup options like baby wipes or paper towels to quickly clean up any spills or messes during travel.

11. Pack any other accessories you might need.

Consider packing any other accessories that your child may need during travel, such as toys, books, or a tablet.

12. Gather all the necessary items now on your emergency kit list.

Before packing anything into the drawer, make sure you've gathered all the necessary items on your emergency kit list.

13. Find the largest items, such as pants or jackets.

The next step is to find the largest items on your list and pack them into the bottom of the bag or backpack.

14. Order your items roughly from largest to smallest.

Order the items roughly from largest to smallest, making sure that you leave enough space for smaller items on top.

15. Grab the diaper bag or backpack.

Grab the diaper bag or backpack and start packing it with the items from your list.

16. Look for the most convenient places in the bag to store appropriate items.

Look for the most convenient places in the bag to store each item, making sure that you organize the items in a way that's easy to access.

17. Purchase and put into the bag a hanging car freshener.

A hanging car freshener can help keep the bag or backpack smelling fresh, even after extended use.

18. Close up the bag or backpack with the zipper or fastening.

Once you've packed everything into the bag or backpack, close it up with the zipper or fastening.

19. Attach the pacifiers, using their clips, to the diaper bag straps.

Attach pacifiers to the bag straps using their clips, so that they're always within reach.

20. Stash the bag or backpack into the drawer in your car.

Finally, stash the bag or backpack into the underseat drawer in your car. This way, you'll always be prepared for any unexpected situations that may arise during travel with your infant or toddler.
