How to Dye Your Hair with Eyeshadow

Dying hair with eyeshadow is a great way to experiment with different hair colors without committing to a permanent dye. Whether you're looking to add a pop of color to your hair for a special occasion or simply want a temporary change, this method is easy and effective. Once you've found a suitable eyeshadow and prepared your hair, all that's left to do is apply the eyeshadow "dye" and wait for the color to take. Follow these steps to learn how to dye your hair with eyeshadow.

Part 1: Gathering Your Materials

1. Find a suitable eyeshadow: The eyeshadow you choose will determine the color of your hair. Look for a shade that complements your skin tone and matches the level of lightness or darkness you want your hair to be. It's best to choose a high-quality eyeshadow that will provide good coverage and pigment.

2. Crush the eyeshadow into a fine powder: Once you've chosen your eyeshadow, use a small brush or the back of a spoon to crush it into a fine powder. This will ensure that the color spreads evenly through your hair.

3. Add warm water to the powdered eyeshadow: Place the powdered eyeshadow into a small bowl, and slowly add warm water until you've created a thick, liquid mixture. It should have the consistency of hair dye.

Part 2: Preparing Your Hair

1. Set up the dyeing area in the bathroom: Because this process can be messy, it's best to set up in your bathroom, near a sink or bathtub. Lay down a towel or plastic sheet to protect your surfaces.

2. Wear old clothes: Dying your hair with eyeshadow can be messy, so it's best to wear old clothes that you don't mind getting stained.

3. Wash your hair first: Before applying the eyeshadow dye, make sure your hair is clean and dry. This will help the color spread evenly and stay in your hair longer.

4. Squeeze as much moisture out of your hair as possible: Use a towel to squeeze as much moisture out of your hair as possible. It shouldn't be soaking wet, but it should still be damp enough to absorb the eyeshadow dye.

Part 3: Applying the Eyeshadow Dye

1. Apply the liquid eyeshadow "dye" onto your clean hair: Dip the brush into the liquid mixture, and apply it directly onto your hair. Use your fingers to work the color in and make sure it's spread evenly.

2. Wear a shower cap to keep your hair up and in contact with the eyeshadow dye: Put on a shower cap to keep your hair up and make sure all of it is in contact with the eyeshadow dye. This will ensure that the color takes evenly and doesn't drip.

3. Wait for any minutes, seconds, or hours to let the color take: The longer you leave the eyeshadow dye in, the deeper and more vivid the color will be. Depending on the intensity you're hoping for, you can leave it on for anywhere from a few minutes to a few hours.

4. Allow your hair to dye: Once you're happy with the amount of time you've left the eyeshadow dye in, wait for it to dye. You can go about your day normally, but be careful not to touch your hair too much or the color may smudge.

5. Wash to remove: When you're ready to remove the eyeshadow dye, simply wash your hair as you normally would. The color will come out easily, and you'll be back to your natural hair color in no time.

6. Set up the dyeing area in the bathroom: Before repeating this process, make sure to thoroughly clean your surfaces and prepare your materials. This will ensure that you don't make a mess or damage any surfaces.

7. Gather together the eyeshadow (one or more colors): If you want to create a multi-tonal effect, gather together a few different eyeshadow colors that complement each other. You can also use just one color for a bold statement.

8. Place all of the eyeshadow you wish to use into the mixing container: Like before, crush all of the eyeshadow into a fine powder and place it into a mixing container.

9. Add other eyeshadow colors if relevant: Depending on the effect you're going for, add other eyeshadow colors to the mixing container. Make sure they blend well together.

10. Add the baby powder: To give the mixture some extra bulk, add a few tablespoons of baby powder to the mixing container. This will help the color spread more easily through your hair.

11. Brush the eyeshadow "dye" onto your hair: Use a brush to apply the eyeshadow "dye" onto your hair. Be careful not to brush too hard, as this can cause the powdered eyeshadow to fly everywhere.

12. Continue brushing in the color until you're happy with the final result: Work the eyeshadow "dye" into your hair until you're happy with the way it looks. Remember that the color will be temporary, so you can experiment freely.

13. Head forth and enjoy the new color for a short time: Now that you've successfully dyed your hair with eyeshadow, it's time to go out and show off your new look! Enjoy the color while it lasts, and have fun experimenting with other shades.

Letter writing is a fundamental skill that everyone should possess. To be able to write proper letters, it is crucial that you know how to write the English alphabet. In this article, you will learn how to make letters of the English alphabet using a piece of lined paper.

Part 1: Getting Started
1. Get a piece of lined paper. Having a piece of lined paper will help you write your letters more accurately and uniformly.

2. Practice the letter "a." Start by placing your pen or pencil at the top of the first line on your paper. Draw a small slanted line downwards to the right, then make a horizontal line across the bottom of the slanted line. Finally, draw another slanted line upwards to the right, connecting it to the end of the horizontal line you drew earlier. Congratulations, you just made the letter "a."

3. Make the letter "b." Start by making a vertical line on the left side of the paper and then curve the line outwards to the right and back inwards to the left to form a small loop. Then, finish the letter by drawing a straight line down to the bottom of the page on the right side of the loop.

4. Try the letter "c." To write this letter, you begin by drawing a circle in an anti-clockwise direction. To write the lower case version of "c," make your circle small and place it on the bottom line of the paper, ensuring it's the same size as the other lowercase letters. Congratulations! You just wrote the letter "c."

5. Make the letter "d." Start by making a circle in an anti-clockwise direction halfway up the line. Extend the circle downward to the bottom line. Then curve the line back upwards and to the right to form a small loop.

6. Try the letter "e." To write the lowercase version of "e," start by drawing a curve from the top left corner of the paper. Continue curving downwards but bend it to the right before curving upwards, then create a small loop and come down again to finish the letter.

7. Make the letter "f." Draw a curve on the top line, almost like the top part of a circle. Draw a vertical line that goes down to the bottom line on the right side of the curve you drew, making it slightly to the right side of the curve.

Part 2: Moving on to More Letters
1. Practice the letter "g." Start with a small "c," then extend a curve downwards towards the bottom line, and curve it back upwards. Loop back down and extend it right across the bottom line, just like you did with "J" and draw it upwards and to the right. But at the top, instead of curving to the left, as you did with "J," go straight down.

2. Try the letter "h." Draw a straight line from the top of the page down to just below halfway. Then curve gently outwards towards the right at the bottom of the line, and curve back inwards to the left and straight back up to join the top line.

3. Make the letter "i." For the lower case "i," write a straight line that goes just above the bottom line, but does not reach the middle line. Additionally, you can add a dot on top of the line.

4. Practice the letter "j." Start by making a hook shape similar to "h" and then add a small loop at the bottom similar to the letter "g." Then, finish the letter "j" by drawing a straight line down to the bottom line.

5. Try the letter "k." Draw a vertical line from the top line down to the middle line, then create two diagonal lines that angle slightly outwards. Extend them just below the middle line and then connect them by drawing a horizontal line in the middle.

6. Make the letter "l." Draw a straight line from the top of the page down to the bottom line, on the right side of the page.

7. Do the letter "m." To write "m," create a straight line from the top of the page to halfway down. Create a loop by curving outwards to the right and inwards to the left. Then, starting to the right of the first loop, create another loop that mirrors the first. Finally, connect the two loops, and close the letter with a straight line down to the bottom of the page.

8. Try the letter "n." Start by creating a vertical line from the top line down to the bottom line. Then, make two curves, one to the right and one to the left, meeting at the middle line. Finally, draw a straight line down to the bottom line similar to the letter "h."

9. Make the letter "o." To create the letter "o", draw a circle in an anti-clockwise direction, ensuring that it is even and circular.

10. Try the letter "p." Make the top part of a circle like "c" and then extend it down to the bottom line, past where the circle finishes and loop it back up to the halfway line. Finally, extend the curve back down towards the bottom line.

11. Practice the letter "q." To write the letter "q," create a curve that starts from the top line and loops toward the bottom line, then curve back upwards to meet the curve above it. Then, add in a small loop that extends from the bottom of the curve and points to the right.

12. Make the letter "r." Draw a straight line from the top of the page down to the bottom line. Create a small curve to the right and a vertical line to the left of the curve.

13. Try the letter "s." Draw the same shape as the uppercase "S," but make it as small as the height of the other lowercase letters.

14. Make the letter "t." To write the lowercase "t," draw a straight line down from the top line to the middle line. Then, add another short horizontal line just underneath the middle line.

15. Try the letter "u." To write the lowercase "u," create a vertical line that extends from the top line down to the middle line. Add a curve that starts from the bottom of the vertical line, curving outward then downwards to the bottom line.

16. Make the letter "v." For the lower case "v," create two diagonal lines that angle slightly outward and connect them at the bottom with a horizontal line.

17. Try the letter "w." There are two ways to write this letter. The first is to create two diagonal lines that angle slightly outward, then add another set at the bottom but angling inward. The second way is to write two connected "u's."

18. Make the letter "x." To write the lowercase "x," create two diagonal lines that cross over each other in the middle.

19. Try the letter "y." For the "y," draw a diagonal line from the top of the page down to the middle line, then create two curved lines extending towards the bottom that meet slightly above the bottom line.

20. Make the letter "z." To create the lowercase "z," draw a diagonal line from the top left corner to the bottom right corner of the paper, then draw a diagonal line from the bottom left to the top right corner and a horizontal line across the bottom to complete the letter.

These are the 26 letters of the English alphabet. With practice and patience, you can become confident in writing all of them in both uppercase and lowercase forms. Keep practicing, and soon you'll be an expert in writing the English alphabet!

Drug addiction is a serious problem that can have severe consequences not only for the user but also for their loved ones. If you are struggling with addiction, you may feel hopeless and helpless. However, there are steps you can take to beat drug addiction. One of the first things you can do is set a goal to quit. In this wikiHow article, we will guide you through 33 steps to help you overcome drug addiction.

1. Set a goal to quit.
One of the first things you can do to overcome drug addiction is to set a goal to quit. Make a commitment to yourself to stop using drugs.

2. Make a list of the harmful effects of your addiction.
Write down all the negative consequences you have experienced as a result of your drug use. This may include physical, social, and legal problems.

3. Write down how you feel physically.
Take note of the physical effects of your drug use. You may experience symptoms such as nausea, headaches, and fatigue.

4. Evaluate if you are neglecting responsibilities.
Consider whether your drug use is interfering with your ability to meet your responsibilities, such as work, school, or family commitments.

5. Think about whether you’ve seen friends or family lately.
If you are isolating yourself from loved ones, it may be time to seek help to get back on track.

6. Admit if you’re stealing or lying to others.
Honesty is an important part of recovery. If you are stealing or lying to obtain drugs or to cover up your drug use, admit it to yourself and seek help.

7. Determine the last time you engaged in a hobby.
Addiction tends to take over your life, leaving little time for hobbies or interests. Consider when you last engaged in a hobby and make a plan to reintegrate these activities into your life.

8. Be honest about how drugs impact your life.
Take a moment to reflect on how drugs have impacted your life. Be honest with yourself about the negative effects and consequences.

9. Write down the positive changes you'll see when you quit.
Make a list of the positive changes you will see in your life when you are no longer using drugs. Focus on these positive changes as a motivator for quitting.

10. See a doctor.
Consulting a medical professional is an important step in overcoming drug addiction. A doctor can evaluate your physical health and recommend appropriate treatment options.

11. Check into a rehabilitation facility.
If you require medical detoxification, you may need to check into a rehabilitation facility.

12. Start seeing a counselor.
Working with a counselor or therapist can help you identify and address the underlying factors that contribute to your addiction.

13. Be open to getting help for different aspects of life.
Sometimes, addiction is a symptom of underlying issues, such as mental health problems or trauma. Be open to getting help for these issues as well.

14. Find a local peer-based support group.
Support groups, such as Alcoholics Anonymous, can provide a supportive environment where you can share your experiences and learn from others.

15. Work with a sponsor.
A sponsor is someone who has been successful in their recovery and can provide guidance and support.

16. Give support to others in your support group.
Helping others in your support group can be a powerful motivator to stay on track with your own recovery.

17. Plan out your day.
Structure and routine can be helpful in overcoming addiction. Make a daily plan and stick to it.

18. Keep track of daily tasks.
Use a planner or smartphone app to keep track of essential tasks and appointments.

19. Be honest with yourself.
Honesty is critical in recovery. Be truthful about your thoughts, feelings, and behaviors.

20. Be patient.
Recovery is a process that takes time. Be patient with yourself and don't beat yourself up if you experience setbacks.

21. Have supportive people around you.
Having supportive friends, family members, or a sponsor can make all the difference in your recovery.

22. Get regular exercise.
Exercise can reduce stress and improve your mood, making it an essential component of recovery.

23. See a nutritionist.
Eating a healthy diet can help support your recovery and improve your physical health.

24. Try yoga.
Yoga can be an effective way to manage stress and improve overall well-being.

25. Try meditation.
Meditation can help you manage stress and reduce cravings.

26. Get acupuncture.
Acupuncture can help reduce cravings and alleviate withdrawal symptoms.

27. See your counselor.
Working with a counselor or therapist regularly can help you maintain your recovery long-term.

28. Create a plan for living without drugs.
Develop a plan for your life without drugs. Identify your goals, and make a plan to achieve them.

29. Make a list of your goals.
What do you want to accomplish in life? Make a list of your goals and prioritize them.

30. Use urge surfing to combat relapse.
Urge surfing involves observing your cravings without acting on them. It can be an effective way to manage cravings.

31. Avoid places and people associated with drugs or drink.
Identify triggers in your life and make a plan to avoid them.

32. Get a job.
Employment can create structure and routine in your life and help build a sense of purpose.

33. Focus on building a new life.
Recovery is an opportunity to build a new, healthier life. Focus on taking small steps to build the life you want, one day at a time.

Disneyland and Walt Disney World are two iconic theme parks known for their classic attractions and immersive experiences. If you are planning a trip to either park, it's important to compare the two to see which one is right for you. Here are some steps to help you compare Disneyland to Walt Disney World.

1. Access information from the Disneyland and Walt Disney World web pages.

Both Disneyland and Walt Disney World have comprehensive websites that provide information about the parks, including attractions, shows, dining, and accommodations. Take some time to explore these pages and learn about what each park has to offer.

2. Reach out and watch some of the videos about each of the resorts on YouTube to learn about these key comparisons.

Watching YouTube videos of both parks can give you a better idea of the overall look and feel of the parks. You can also see footage of the attractions and shows to get a better sense of what to expect.

3. Read up about the key comparisons via other websites including third party fan sites and Wikipedia.

There are many websites and fan forums devoted to Disneyland and Walt Disney World. These sites can provide valuable insights into the differences between the two parks, including insider tips and recommendations.

4. Scroll around the areas near each park system on Google Earth and see what information will make you smile.

Use Google Earth to explore the areas surrounding both Disneyland and Walt Disney World. You may discover nearby attractions or restaurants that could help sway your decision.

5. Ask other paying guests who visited both parks recently (whether that be other family members who have been to the parks recently, or other people who you don't know but have knowledge about both parks' comparisons.

Reach out to friends or family members who have visited both parks to ask for their opinions. You can also connect with other fans on social media to get their thoughts on the park comparison.

6. Look into the location of each park.

Consider the location of each park and how easy it is to get to. Disneyland is located in Anaheim, California, while Walt Disney World is located in Orlando, Florida. Depending on where you live, one park may be more convenient for you to get to.

7. Put some serious thought behind the amount of parks for each park system.

Walt Disney World is made up of four separate theme parks, while Disneyland has two parks. Think about which parks you want to visit and how much time you have to spend at each park.

8. Research to learn the different parking type scenarios for each parking area involved.

Both parks offer parking options, but they differ in terms of cost and location. Research these parking options to decide which one works best for your visit.

9. Research how far away one park is to another within each park system.

If you plan on visiting multiple parks within the same system, consider the distance between each park and how much time you need to travel between them.

10. Locate how many hotel resorts exist nearby each park system that are specifically tied to the Disney Parks Co.

Both Disneyland and Walt Disney World have several hotels located near the parks. Look at the options available and consider which one fits your needs and budget.

11. Grab a park map of all the parks between all of the two park systems and try to learn about some of the theme park attractions that comprise both park systems.

Study the park maps of both Disneyland and Walt Disney World to plan your visit. Look at the attractions and shows and decide which ones you want to prioritize.

12. Although both parks have the big attractions, some of the biggest attractions have small differences that only those who visit or who watch some of the videos of the on-ride (POV) ride-thrus say "Yes, that's one!"

Pay attention to the subtle differences between attractions at both parks. These small differences can help you decide which park to visit.

13. Recognize that both of the Disneyland parks have nighttime shows and premier them in conjunction with each other so that guests can exit one show and have a few breathing moments before the other show starts.

Nighttime shows are a highlight of both Disneyland and Walt Disney World. Be sure to plan your day accordingly to make sure you don't miss these spectacular performances.

14. Look forward to plenty of shopping and food establishments at both park systems.

Both Disneyland and Walt Disney World offer a wide variety of shops and restaurants. Take some time to explore the options and find your favorites.

Comparing Disneyland to Walt Disney World can help you make an informed decision about which park to visit. By following these steps, you can ensure you have a magical experience no matter which park you choose.

, You'll need a wood board, sanded and smooth. You'll also need paint, varnish (or another clear coating), and a soldering iron (with a calligraphy tip or similar). Pencil and paper will also help.

, Soldering irons are a little tricky. Practice on some scrap wood first so that you can see how slow you need to go and how hard you need to press. It will also give you the chance to figure how to move it in order to get different shapes.

, Make sure the wood is clean and smooth.

, You can draw the design free-hand or you can use a "cheat", such as printing the characters onto transfer paper or you can purchase ink stamps from a craft store.


Make sure the designs are not too small or detailed, especially if you do not have a lot of experience with a soldering iron.

, Draw over the design with the iron. Don't rush yourself. Look at it in different lights to be sure that it looks right.

, Let the burned areas cool and then brush off any excess material. Erase pencil marks and other marks and gently wipe the whole surface with a lightly damp cloth. Let it dry before going on.

, The varnish or other clear coating will create the smooth surface necessary to make the planchette work. Carefully follow the manufacturer's instructions for whatever finish you decide to use. You will likely need to do several coats and each one takes a long time to dry correctly. Set aside plenty of time for this project.

, You can paint on even more details once the varnish has dried completely. Details are best painted on in the second to last layer of varnish, so long as it is clear and not colored.

, There are a number of ways that you can lay out your board. The design isn't so important, so long as you have all of the necessary symbols. You need to include at least the letters of the alphabet, numbers 0-9, and the words "yes","no", and "farewell".


Use the traditional layout if you want to. This is where the letters are written out in two slightly-arced lines at the center, with yes/no above and the numbers and farewell below.
You could also use a diamond layout. In this design, the letters are written in a diamond shape, with each of the four points lining up with the center of that side of the board. The numbers are written inside this diamond and the corners are allocated for the words (allowing for an additional phrase, like "not now").
You could also try a circular layout. This design is similar to the diamond, but uses an oval shape instead.

, Choose a font which fits with the design of your ouija board. Gothic fonts are good choices, as are many Western-themed fonts. You can, however, choose whatever you like best. Write some test text in a word editor to get an idea of what you think looks best.

, Many believe it's a good idea to add certain symbols to the corners or other location on your board. Beliefs vary, but common symbols include: the moon, sun, and stars; the symbols for the elements; the symbols for the planets; and symbols or images in neurologically significant quantities (12, 13, 7, 3, etc).


You can also embellish your board with physical objects, such as set spots for candles, attached stones (quartz is good for this purpose), or places to put the elements (a bowl of water, a branch, etc).

, Choose a color scheme for your board. Darker, earth tones tend to be better at calling forth the spirits, but if you're looking to contact particularly cheerful spirits, there's nothing wrong with using colors you think they'd prefer. If you're trying to contact someone in particular, try their favorite color.


Try to use colors that contrast well. You want to be able to see your text easily.

, You'll need a planchette (the device where you place your hands) in order to use your ouija board. You can rescue one from a random set you find at a dollar store, or you can make your own.

Prepare yourself for the rigors of years of medical training by taking advanced placement classes during high school. While these type of classes are not available at all high schools, and are not a requirement of getting a Pre-Med degree, they will put you on good footing when starting college.


It's important to develop a work ethic early. Advanced placement classes will help you get a foundation understanding of complex topics but they will also help you become used to the extended periods of study you will need to complete during your medical education.
AP credits in high school may even count as college credits in some cases. Remember, however, that those same credits, while they may help you get your undergraduate degree, may not count toward the prerequisites required to get into medical school.;
, A premed degree will include most, if not all, of the required prerequisites to get into medical school. Classes taken should include a wide variety of topics in the sciences, especially biology, in addition to classes in English, Math, and other general requirements.


If you choose not to major in premed you can still go to medical school. It just means that you will have to complete required classes for medical school, in addition to those required for your major.Prerequisites for pediatric college include at least 8 credit hours each of Biology, general or Inorganic Chemistry, Organic Chemistry, Physics, and English. All of the Science classes also need to include a lab.Make sure that your undergraduate education includes all of these prerequisites.

, Even more than what undergraduate degree you get, medical schools are looking at MCAT scores to judge whether you should be admitted.The MCAT is designed to test "problem solving, critical thinking, and knowledge of natural, behavioral, and social science concepts and principles."The MCAT consists of four multiple-choice sections. The first section is "Biological and Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems," the second section is "Chemical and Physical Foundations of Biological Systems," the third section is "Psychological, Social, and Biological Foundations of Behavior," and the fourth section is "Critical Analysis and Reasoning Skills."The full test is 230 questions and takes a little over four hours to complete.
Studying for the MCAT is an important step toward passing the exam. You can either buy published study guides or use online study guides and practice tests. Either way, you need to devote a lot of your time to studying leading up the the test.

,, This can be done on the website of the American Association of Colleges of Pediatric Medicine.With one application on their website you can apply to all nine colleges of podiatric medicine at the same time.


Applicants to podiatry school with be evaluated on their GPA, MCAT scores, and an interview, in addition to a variety of extracurricular activities. Experiences in the medical field, such as volunteer work or shadowing a podiatrist are good to have as extracurricular activities, although the requirements for admission can vary from school to school., These requirements will vary slightly from school to school but all will be very similar to any medical schooling, aside from the focus on the foot. Topics will include anatomy, physiology, and pharmacology, among others. In addition, students will need to learn about medical ethics and regulations about the practice of podiatry.


Podiatric medical school will also include hands on training. Students will need to complete several clinical rotations before graduating. These rotations are usually completed during the last two years of the program., The second part, covering clinical areas, is taken just prior to graduation.


As of 2015, the complete APMLE consists of three parts, consisting of four sections.The first two sections, considered one part, are written, testing that the candidate has the scientific and medical knowledge to work as a physician. The third section of the test evaluates the candidate's clinical skills, focusing on their skill at examining and communicating with patients.

, Residency allows future podiatrists to apply the knowledge they have learned in school in the real world.It also allows them to practice the skills they have learned in school with supervision.


In comparison to the small number of schools where you can get a DPM degree, there are quite a few residencies you can do in podiatry.Approved residencies in podiatry can be found through the American Association of Colleges of Podiatric Medicine and the Council on Pediatric Medical Education.The residencies listed on the websites of these organizations have gone through the approval process of the American Pediatric Medical Association., While residency should give all future podiatrists some experience in a variety of specializations, you can specialize in a specific aspect of podiatry. Podiatrists can become specialized in two different fields, primary care and orthopedics, or surgery.Picking a specialty requires specialized training and passing exams in the specialized field. National specialty boards then grant certification., This is the final step in becoming a podiatrist. Passing the test allows you to obtain a license to practice podiatry.The fourth part tests the clinical skills of the candidate, gauging their skills at evaluating, diagnosing, and treating patients.
Requirements vary from state to state. Your state may require you to take an additional state specific test in order to practice podiatry. Check with your local licensing board for specific details.
Remember that your license will need to be renewed periodically. Don't accidentally let it lapse!

How to Present an Oral Argument

Presenting an oral argument in a courtroom can be a daunting task, but with the right preparation, you can make a strong case. Whether you’re an experienced attorney or representing yourself, it’s important to understand the purpose of oral argument, review the rules, and thoroughly read all relevant materials. Once you’ve outlined your argument and practiced your delivery, you’ll be ready to stand before the court and make your case. Here’s how:

1. Understand the purpose of oral argument.

Before you begin preparing your argument, it’s important to understand why oral argument is necessary. In general, the purpose of oral argument is to help judges understand the key issues and arguments in a case, and to ask any questions they may have. It’s an opportunity to highlight the strengths of your position and address any weaknesses.

2. Find the court’s list of rules.

Every court has specific rules and guidelines for oral argument, so make sure you review them carefully before you prepare your argument. These rules may cover everything from dress code to time limits and procedures for submitting exhibits. By understanding the rules in advance, you’ll avoid any surprises in the courtroom.

3. Re-read the briefs and the record.

Before you begin outlining your argument, review the briefing and the court record to refresh your memory on the relevant facts and legal issues. This will help you identify any gaps in your knowledge and ensure that you’re fully prepared to address the court’s questions.

4. Re-read all legal authority cited by the parties.

In addition to reviewing the facts, it’s important to review all legal authority cited by the parties, including cases, statutes, and regulations. This will help you address any potential weaknesses in your argument and anticipate any counterarguments.

5. Outline your argument.

Once you’ve reviewed all the materials, outline your argument. This should include a brief summary of the facts, the legal issues at stake, and your key arguments in support of your position. Be sure to organize your arguments in a clear, logical fashion.

6. Create a “cheat sheet” of important facts and legal authority.

To help you stay organized during your argument, create a “cheat sheet” that includes key facts and legal authority. This will enable you to quickly reference important information without having to flip through your materials.

7. Draft a list of questions and answers.

As you outline your argument, anticipate the questions that the judges may ask. Draft a list of potential questions and prepare concise, clear answers.

8. Practice.

Practice delivering your argument out loud, ideally in front of a trusted colleague or mentor who can give you feedback. Rehearsing your argument will help you feel more confident and polished on the day of your oral argument.

9. Listen to sample arguments.

To prepare even further, listen to sample oral arguments online or in person. This will help you understand the pacing and tone of a successful argument and give you more ideas on how to format your presentation.

10. Dress appropriately.

On the day of your argument, dress in a way that is professional and respectful of the court. This will show the judges that you take your case seriously and are prepared to make a strong argument.

11. Turn off your cell phone.

In order to avoid any distractions during your argument, turn off your cell phone or any other devices that might produce noise.

12. Find where you are supposed to sit.

Before the court session begins, locate the position where you will be presenting your argument. This will help you feel more comfortable and prepared when it’s your turn to speak.

13. Speak confidently into the microphone.

Most courtrooms have a microphone to amplify your voice. Speak clearly and confidently into the microphone so that judges can hear your arguments.

14. Introduce yourself to the court.

When you begin your argument, introduce yourself and state your role in the case. Be polite and respectful to the court.

15. Reserve time, if you are the appellant.

If you are the party appealing the case, it’s important to reserve some time for rebuttal at the end of oral arguments. This will allow you to address any new issues that may have arisen during the other party’s presentation.

16. State your requested relief and the grounds.

After you introduce yourself, state your requested relief and the grounds for your request. This should be a concise summary of your argument.

17. Avoid a lengthy recitation of facts.

While it’s important to have a strong grasp of the facts, avoid reciting them at length during your argument. Focus on the key legal issues, rather than getting bogged down in details.

18. Look at the judges.

Maintain eye contact with the judges while you’re speaking. This will help you build a connection with them and show that you’re confident in your arguments.

19. Stand still.

While it can be tempting to pace or fidget, try to stand still while you’re presenting. This will help you appear confident and composed.

20. Try to avoid using exhibits.

In most cases, it’s best to rely on your oral argument rather than long exhibits. This will help you keep the pace of your argument moving and avoid losing the judges’ attention.

21. Raise all of your arguments.

During your argument, make sure to raise all of your key arguments. This will help you ensure that you’ve covered all relevant issues and strengthen your overall position.

22. Understand the question before answering.

If a judge asks you a question, take a moment to understand the specific issue they’re raising before answering. This will help you avoid confusion or misunderstandings.

23. Answer the question asked.

When you’re answering a judge’s question, focus on the specific issue they’ve raised rather than trying to steer the conversation back to your main argument.

24. Correct inaccuracies and mis-impressions.

If a judge has a misunderstanding of the facts or legal issues, be sure to correct it clearly and politely.

25. Don’t talk over the judge.

While it’s important to make your case, it’s also important to respect the judges’ authority. Don’t talk over them or interrupt them while they’re speaking.

26. Offer supplemental briefing.

If there’s an issue that you weren’t able to fully address during oral argument, offer to provide supplemental briefing to the court.

27. Listen closely.

As other parties present their arguments, listen closely to what they’re saying. This will help you identify potential weaknesses in your own argument or anticipate questions from the judges.

28. List the appellee’s strongest arguments.

During your rebuttal period, list the appellee’s strongest arguments and provide a concise rebuttal.

29. Jot down counterarguments.

During oral argument, take notes on any counterarguments that may arise so you’re prepared to address them.

30. Rank the arguments in importance.

Prioritize the arguments you’ve outlined based on their strength and importance.

31. Summarize the argument, then rebut.

At the end of oral argument, summarize the key points of your argument and then provide a concise rebuttal to the other party’s position.

By following these steps, you’ll be well on your way to presenting a strong and effective oral argument in court. Remember to stay calm, focused, and respectful, and you’ll increase your chances of success.

, There are treble recorders for any budget, both plastic and wooden. A wooden one will have much nicer tone, but it will cost a lot more money. If you're starting out, it's probably best to buy a plastic one, in case you decide at a later date that you don't like it. An additional concern when buying is that a plastic one will remain to be useful when you upgrade (it's very good for practicing on), whereas a cheaper wooden one will not. It's always best to buy the best you can afford, but a good plastic will be a better option than a cheap wooden one.

, Unlike the descant (soprano) recorder, the treble will invariably come disassembled in a case. It comes in three parts: the the head joint (into which you blow), the body (which contains most of the fingering holes), and the foot joint. Ensure that the foot joint is skewed slightly to the right, so that when your fingers cover all of the holes, your little finger rests easily over the final hole.

, Your left thumb should cover the hole on the underside of the body and your middle three fingers should cover the holes on top. Your little finger should be free. The thumb of your right hand should balance the recorder and the remaining fingers should cover the remaining holes.

, This is the note E. Try getting someone to play that note on the piano. If you're higher than the piano, you're blowing too hard and if you're lower then you're blowing too softly. Experiment until you find the right amount to blow.

, Before playing any note, pronounce a "doo" sound, so that your tongue touches the roof of your mouth. This produces a much clearer note.

, Once again, check if you're blowing the right amount by testing the note on a piano.

, Check if you're blowing the right amount by testing the note on a piano again, but you should be starting to get a feel for the amount you need to blow to play a note in tune.

, Play a C, then put the forefinger and middle finger of your right hand on their holes. You should have 5 holes (and the underneath hole) covered.

, This note requires less air pressure than previous notes, so make sure that you don't blow too hard.

, This note requires still less air pressure than G, so make sure that you don't blow too hard. This is the lowest note on the treble recorder.

, Moving from E to F (as is common) takes some getting used to, and is quite difficult to get smooth. Practice it. You will learn an alternative fingering for E later to accommodate this, but if possible, always try to use the E fingering above. High F is also known as F' .

, You may have wondered why B was missed out earlier. This is because Bb makes up the F major scale, and is therefore taught before B (since the treble recorder is an "F" recorder). Further, it is more difficult because it is a "forked note", meaning the middle finger is off the hole, but the forefinger and ring finger are on the holes. So, play a low F and remove the middle finger of your right hand.

, Simply play F, G, A, Bb, C, D, E, F' and then back down.

, This will take some practice to make sure that the recorder is properly balanced and that you get a nice even tone. Try playing it against the piano, to check tuning. High G is also known as G' .

, Play a High G and then add your forefinger, remembering to keep your thumb off its hole. A very common trill is F# to G, and on recorder, this is exceptionally easy. Just quickly take your forefinger off the hole and put it back, without tonguing each time. High F# is also known as F#' .

, Play a note G and remove the forefinger of your right hand. You can trill between C and B really easily by just pulling your middle and ring fingers up and down quickly without tonguing.

, Simply play G, A, B, C, D, E, F#', G' and then back down.

, Play a note E and add the middle finger of your left hand and the forefinger of your right hand.

, Play G, A, Bb, C, D, E, F#', G' going up and G', F', Eb, D, C, Bb, A, G going down.

, To get high notes, one must employ a technique called "pinching", which involves your thumb. Simply slip the tip of your thumb inside the thumb hole. Practice moving your thumb in this way, between pinched and non-pinched, because you will have to do this a lot.

, Play an A, but instead of covering the hole with your thumb, pinch it, as explained above. It should sound an octave higher than the lower A. Practice moving between A and High A (A'), remembering to tongue every time. Check your tuning on the piano.

, Play a G but remove your thumb and the forefinger of your left hand. This note is quite difficult, but necessary for the next two scales.

, Play A, B, C, D, E, F#', G#', A' going up, then A', G', F', E, D, C, B, A going down.

, Play an A and remove the ring finger of your left hand. Then, notice that there are two holes under the ring finger of your right hand. Cover the rightmost one of these. This will take some practice. Try moving from C# to D. Check your tuning on a piano.

, Play A, B, C#, D, E, F#', G#', A' and then back down again.

, Play a High A, remove the middle finger of your right hand and add your right hand's ring finger. Remember not to add your right hand's little finger as you did with regular Bb.

, Play Bb, C, D, Eb, F', G', A', Bb' and then back down again.

Building stairs may seem like a daunting task, but with careful planning and execution, it can be accomplished by nearly anyone. Whether you're building a staircase for a deck, a loft, or an addition to your home, the process remains largely the same. From measuring to finishing, here's a comprehensive guide on how to create stairs for your specific needs.

Measure the height of the area where you will install the stairs.

The first step in building stairs is to measure the height of the area where you'll install them. This measurement is known as the total rise.

Divide the total rise by the typical rise per step.

Next, divide the total rise by the typical rise per step. While the typical rise per step is around 7 inches (17.8 cm), it's essential to check your local building codes to see if there are any specific regulations you need to follow.

Divide the total rise by the number of steps to get the actual rise per step.

After you have divided the total rise by the typical rise per step, you need to divide the total rise again by the number of steps you want to create. The result will give you the actual rise per step.

Establish the run of each step to get the total run of the staircase.

The run of each step refers to the horizontal measurement of the staircase. Multiply the number of steps by the tread depth (typically 10 inches or 25.4 cm) to determine the total run.

Calculate the length of the stringers.

Stringers are the structural supports for staircases. You'll need to calculate the length of the stringers and the number required for your particular staircase. You can use an online calculator to estimate stringer length based on the total rise and run of your staircase.

Determine how you will attach the stairs to the existing structure.

The next step is to determine how you will attach the stairs to the existing structure. Depending on your situation, you may need to use lag bolts, brackets, or other hardware.

Determine how many stringers you will need.

The number of stringers you need will depend on the width and span of your staircase. Typically, you'll need at least two stringers for a standard staircase.

Lay out a sufficiently long piece of 2x12-inch lumber.

Lay out a lengthy piece of 2x12-inch (5cm x 3m) lumber to create the stringers. Make sure it's long enough to fit the entire length of the stringer.

Mark a framer's square to the height and depth of your steps.

With a framer's square, mark the height and depth of each step onto the lumber.

Modify the top of the stringer to account for the overall angle.

Using an angle grinder or circular saw, modify the top of each stringer to account for the overall angle of the staircase.

Measure and mark each step along the piece of lumber.

Using a tape measure, mark out each step along the piece of lumber. Make sure the markings are evenly spaced.

Use a circular saw or hand saw to cut the step-notches into the stringers.

At each marking, use a circular saw or hand saw to cut step-notches into the stringers.

Square off the bottom of the stringer so that it will sit flush with the ground.

At the bottom of each stringer, square off the end to ensure that it sits flush with the ground.

Test the stringer by setting it in place.

Test the stringer by setting it in place. Make any necessary adjustments before moving forward.

Use the first stringer as a template off of which to base the next stringers.

After determining that the first stringer is secure and level, use it as a template for the remaining stringers.

Install the stringers.

Position the stringers in place, secure them to the structure, and use a level to ensure they're straight.

Secure and stabilize the stringers by installing risers, or toe boards (optional).

If desired, install risers or toe boards to add extra stability and safety.

Cut and install the risers.

Cut and install the risers with a saw and secure them to the stringers with screws or nails.

Cut and install the treads.

Cut and install the treads with nails or screws, and make sure they are level and flush with each other.

Fit any trim board underneath the overhanging tread and nail to the riser.

For a finished look, add a trim board underneath the overhanging tread and nail it to the riser.

Varnish, paint, or seal your steps if necessary.

Finally, complete your staircase by varnishing, painting, or sealing the steps if necessary. This step will help to protect your stairs and make them look great for years to come.

Are you tired of using the same old default ringtones on your phone? Do you want to create a unique bell ringtone using Audacity? Look no further! With Audacity, a free and open-source audio editing software, you can create a customized bell ringtone to impress your friends and family. Follow these steps to make a bell ringtone using Audacity:

1. Download and install the latest version of Audacity Beta and LAME MP3 Encoder.

Before you get started, you'll need to download and install the latest versions of Audacity Beta and LAME MP3 Encoder. These programs are essential for creating and exporting your bell ringtone.

2. Open Audacity and resize the window to fill the entire screen.

Once you've installed Audacity Beta and LAME MP3 Encoder, open Audacity and make sure the window is resized to fill the entire screen.

3. Generate the bell tone.

To generate the bell tone, select the Generate menu item and choose Tone. Set the frequency to 1000 Hz and the amplitude to 0.5. Click OK to create the tone on a new track.

4. Deselect the track: go to None or click on the gray area below the track(s) above.

To work on the other tracks, deselect the current track. You can do this by going to the None option in the track dropdown or by clicking on the gray area below the other tracks.

5. Repeat this step using a frequency of 1500 Hz and an amplitude of 0.33, and then with a frequency of 2800 Hz and an amplitude of 0.56. Each time, a new track should appear.

Repeat the previous step two more times, but with different frequencies and amplitudes for each track. This will create a multi-tone bell ringtone.

6. Select all three tracks.

Once you've created all three tracks, select them all by clicking Edit and then Select.

7. Apply a tremolo effect.

Apply a tremolo effect to all three tracks by clicking Effect and then Tremolo. Set the waveform type to "inverse sawtooth" and the wetness level to 100%.

8. Save your work.

Save your work by choosing File and then Save Project.

9. Add a pause between rings.

Add a pause between rings by selecting a region of silence in Audacity and copying and pasting it at the end of the ringtone.

10. Save under a different filename.

Save your work under a different filename to avoid overwriting your original project.

11. Eliminate clicks.

Eliminate any clicks or pops in the ringtone by zooming in and editing any spiky or harsh peaks.

12. Quiet down the ringtone.

Quiet down the ringtone by selecting the Amplify option and reducing the sound levels until it's at an appropriate volume.

13. Test the ringtone using Audacity's loop playback feature.

Test the ringtone using Audacity's loop playback feature (Transport and then Loop Play). This will play the ringtone repeatedly so you can hear how it sounds.

14. Adjust the pitch (optional).

If the pitch doesn't sound quite right, adjust it to your liking by selecting Effect and then Change Pitch.

15. Save your project once more under a different filename.

Save your project one last time under a different filename.

16. Export as a WAV file (for converting).

Export your project as a WAV file to convert it to a compatible format for your phone.

17. Export as an MP3 file (most phones, except the iPhone).

Export your project as an MP3 file, which is compatible with most phones except for iPhones.

18. Upload your ringtone to your phone.

Finally, upload your customized bell ringtone to your phone and enjoy!

19. Tweak the loudness.

If your ringtone isn't loud enough compared to other ringtones on your phone, you can tweak the loudness using the Amplify effect before exporting it. Make sure it's comparable to other ringtones on your phone. 

Congratulations! You have successfully created your very own bell ringtone using Audacity. With some creativity and experimentation, you can create all sorts of custom ringtones to your liking.

In order to open a Dunkin’ Donuts franchise, you must have a net worth of $500,000 and at least $250,000 in liquid assets.Having the necessary capital available is essential for applying to become a Dunkin’ Donuts franchise owner.The requirements for international franchising are slightly different, requiring at least $1,500,000 in liquid assets and $3,000,000 in minimum net worth as well as the intention and capital required to develop at least 20 restaurants.;
, While you will be required to pay an initial franchising fee that ranges from $40,000 to $90,000 (depending on the type of unit you are looking to franchise), the costs will not stop there.You will also need to consider building and real estate costs, the cost of equipment and signs, the costs of licenses and permits, the cost of uniforms, the cost of insurance, etc.Expect to spend between $134,600 and $1,611,100 as your initial investment for a new restaurant.This does not include real estate costs.

, Dunkin’ Donuts looks for candidates for franchising that have prior restaurant management or food service operations experience.In addition, they look for candidates that demonstrate the ability to recruit, train, maintain, and motivate a high-performing team as well as experience managing profit and loss operations.Finally, they also want applicants who have an understanding of real estate development.You will want to thoroughly evaluate your prior business experience before applying to become a Dunkin’ Donuts franchise owner.

,, Dunkin’ Donuts expects their franchisors to exhibit superior character.They focus on several key qualities found to be important to successful franchising. These qualities include: honesty, transparency, humility, fairness, respect, responsibility, and integrity.Before applying to open a Dunkin’ Donuts franchise, it is important to evaluate your own interaction with these values to make sure you will be a good fit for the company and the brand.


Ask yourself: are these values important to me? If possible, evaluate the ways in which you exemplify these values, particularly in the workplace. This will help you get a position as a Dunkin’ Donuts franchiser.

, You will want to look at the market availability for Dunkin’ Donuts franchises and see if there are available markets in your location of interest before proceeding with the franchising application. Markets are available in both the USA and Canada, with “highlighted markets” of particular interest in California, Minnesota, Michigan, Missouri, and Louisiana..


There are some locations considered “reserved markets.” This includes most of New England, Pennsylvania, Florida, Nevada, Delaware, Maryland, and Washington, DC.This means that the markets are reserved for existing franchisees.
Some locations (including Washington, Oregon, Idaho, Montana, Wyoming, Nebraska, and Alaska are considered to be “future markets.”These are markets in which Dunkin’ Donuts has not opened yet and in which there are currently no development opportunities.
There are also opportunities for international franchising. Dunkin’ Donuts has franchises in over 60 countries and is looking for additional growth around the world.,, You can access the application for domestic franchising here: http://franchisinginformation.dunkinbrands.com/Application. The application for international franchising is accessible at this site: http://dunkinbrands.force.com/internationalapplication.

, In particular, the application will ask if you want to open a “traditional store” or a “non-traditional store.” Non-traditional stores are unique venues (including airports, mass transit terminals, stadiums, hospitals, grocery stores, casinos, military bases, and universities) in which you can open a Dunkin’ Donuts store.These non-traditional sites are designed to be placed in high-traffic venues or places where people dwell for extended times or are “captured audiences.”, You will be asked questions about your approximate total net worth and the approximate amount of your liquid assets.Next, you will be asked to discuss (in a free-form paragraph) your relevant restaurant experience.


You will also have the opportunity to attach a resume. This will be helpful if you have experience that you feel will be attractive to those reviewing your application.
You will be asked if you have a corporation (and if so, to provide information about it) and will be asked to list any additional partners., After providing this information, you will have the option of reviewing and submitting your application. Your application will be reviewed by the Dunkin’ Donuts franchise team.You will be emailed a confirmation receipt upon reception of your online application.This email will include details regarding the next steps in the application review process.

, You will receive the Franchise Disclosure Document (FDD), which contains information about the franchisor, the franchise system, and the agreements that will be required to open a franchise.Specifically, the FDD contains Dunkin Donuts financial statements, the franchise agreement, franchisee contacts, cost breakdowns, initial franchise fees, royalties and advertising fees. This is designed to assist in you in making an informed decision about opening a Dunkin’ Donuts franchise.

, You will be required to produce proof of your citizenship as an American citizen or provide your Permanent Resident/Alien Registration Card.In addition, you will be asked to provide proof of your assets so that the company can ensure that you have the financial resources and stability required to open a franchise. You will also be required to submit to a credit and background check as well., After your initial screening (done through the application) is complete, you will meet with the franchise manager.Through communication with the franchise manager as well as other franchise owners, you will begin to understand how to develop a business plan for your Dunkin’ Donuts franchise, which is an essential part of becoming a franchise owner.


Consider what questions you may want to ask to the franchise manager and to other franchise owners. In particular, other franchise owners will be able to provide you with their personal experiences owning a Dunkin’ Donuts franchise and may be able to provide advice on what to do and what not to do.

, Your business plan is an important part of demonstrating your ability to run a successful Dunkin’ Donuts franchise. You will be required to submit this business plan to the franchise manager.Your financial review will be done based on the information that you include in this business plan. You will find out more about the detailed requirements of the business plan as you go through the application process, but it should include the following general information:


Details about the local market where you will open your franchise.
Information about competitors in your area.
Facts about what you will bring to the company and your expertise, including previous experience in the restaurant industry.
Your financial model for managing your franchise.

, A thorough review of your financial plan for your franchise will be conducted based on your business plan. You must have a detailed financial model for running your franchise to present to the franchise manager. As long as you have included a detailed financial plan in your business plan, you will not have to do anything more as you undergo review.

, You will receive franchise approval once your financial and background checks are completed, after you have had a financial review, and after you have completed and submitted your business plan. Approval will only be given to candidates who meet all the requirements of franchise owners.

, This agreement sets out the geographic areas for the development of your new Dunkin’ Donuts restaurant or restaurants.When you sign the SDA, you are agreeing to develop the minimum number of restaurants specified for that location.This number is determined by Dunkin’ Donuts.

, Before you open your first restaurant, you will be required to attend a 3-day training course held in Boston, Massachusetts.After completing this course, you must complete the Dunkin’ Donuts Core Initial Training program. This program is held in Braintree, Massachusetts or Orlando, Florida or in a designated training restaurant.More details regarding the training required by Dunkin’ Donuts can be found in the FDD., These programs are designed to teach you about the brand and about restaurant management in general.Topics that will be covered include an overview of Dunkin’ Brands, information about restaurant management systems, and an explanation of manufacturing and production., These professionals will help you to identify and develop your new restaurant’s location. The demographics and lease requirements for a Dunkin’ Donuts restaurant can be found here: http://www.dunkinfranchising.com/content/dam/franchisee/Process/sitereuirements/DD-Site-Demo-Lease-Requirements.pdf.


Pay attention to the specific requirements for preferred demographics and traffic count. In addition, note the typical lease requirements and make sure that you are able to abide by these terms before agreeing to open a franchise.

, Dunkin’ Donuts will require you to abide by certain terms and meet certain minimums in your restaurant. For example, there are specific requirements for electricity, the HVAC system, the water piping, utilities, and restrooms.All of these requirements can be found here: http://www.dunkinfranchising.com/content/dam/franchisee/Process/sitereuirements/DD-Site-Demo-Lease-Requirements.pdf.

You can offer prizes or incentives for attending your grand opening. For example, you may want to reward the first customer at your drive-through with a gift card to Dunkin’ Donuts. This can help you gain and keep customers.
